speaker: interviewer
text: I asked you while we were changing reels if you actually ever witnessed what you just described at the end of the last reel. Do you want to talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I witnessed interrogation, but not to such an extent. I witnessed interrogation when people were forced to tell him this-- the interrogation I witnessed, they were mostly money, wealth. Where is your money? Where did you hide your wealth? Where is the-- your gold? We know you were wealthy. You tell us, where is your money? Now, if this was a-- the policy, official policy, of the-- of the SS or this was a personal question from this certain officer, I don't know. But I seen this, for instance. And they-- actually, what they did-- they bring in the family of the man. And if they have a small child, they told him, if you love your child. He said, yes. I give you five minutes. Either you tell me where your money is, or your child will be dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard this. I was there. The man said everything what you want to know. People-- the next time I was there, people were telling people about hiding pieces. Fortunately, nobody know about mine. But people said, that they look for a certain person, you know. We know you know where it is. You were friends. We have to find him. Where it is? And I says, I don't know. He said, we know you know. We want you to-- They brought his wife in. And they said, do you want to know this-- we take your wife out and gave us pleasure? And the man said, I don't know. I don't know nothing, absolutely nothing. He said, take her out, OK? And whatever happened to her, I don't know, because I left in this point. And I don't know if the man really know where was their friend or what they did to his wife. I has absolutely no idea. But see, it's-- a lot of time, it's very hard to put yourself on somebody else's position. Like, I know a case when the headquarters put in a well-known Jewish leader. I can't remember his name because I wasn't involved in this. And they wanted to know all kind of information regarding you know, their connections and everything about it, you know? Told him, either you speak and give away these people, you know, who are doing certain things, or who are in the underground, or-- or did certain acts against the Germans or the Hungarian Arrow Cross people, or we deport your family to Auschwitz. Now, what would you do, as a man? I mean, I'm just asking you a logical question. You-- you know, I am a married man. I have five children. If somebody would tell me this question, what would you say? What would I have said? I love my family. Would you cooperate with them, with the hope that you save your family? Or you don't give a darn about your family? Now, it's a very interesting question, this, you know? And I seen this happen. And I seen this happen. And the people talked to save their family. I don't know-- by the way, I don't know if they really saved this, their families. They were told they will be saved. I don't know. Eichmann and his people-- they were very good in this, you know, doing all this. But again, I never seen Eichmann do anything personally, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the man who gave the orders. But personally, I never seen him personally do anything. And when I seen the pictures from Adolph Eichmann, when the Eichmann trial came on, I don't recognize the same man, for a simple reason. He was always well-dressed, you know, the puffed cheek and a German military uniform. And they showed me a picture with him, you know, a skinny little guy with bald high. I mean, I won't recognize my own brother, you know, after some-- in this condition, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I told him, you  know, he looks like him. But if I have to swear for it, I wouldn't say it. Because I-- remember, I seen only these people in uniform. And you see a person in uniform, and you see next time without the uniform, they don't look the same. I mean, you have to know the person to recognize him. And after-- after so many years, when people change-- and it just happened to me recently. I was-- where I was? I was playing golf somewhere, you know? And a guy came to me. He said, you're Les Banos, aren't you? I told him, yes. He said, oh, I'm Harry Schmidt. I told him, hi. And the name get me this-- and for a second-- to save my life, I would not recall when I knew the guy, where I was with him. He said, we used to work at WTAE together. And I-- and I seen him always just regular street clothes, wasn't even uniform. And I could not remember. And he remembered me. Apparently, I did not change. Some people don't change, you know. Do I look-- do I look to you like a 73-years-old man? You know, as far as the university think, they think I'm 64. When you saw-- when you saw Eichmann--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and your experience crossing paths, was there ever any-- was there ever a time when you had to do something that you didn't want to do, to prove to the SS that you were a loyal SS? No. I never had to do anything. Because I was assigned as a liaison between the Hungarian and the German Army-- I mean, the SS and the Hungarian Army. And as such, I never had anything except convey information or gave him orders, which came in to move, where troop movements, or daily routine things, you know. But I had never, ever was told to do anything special, you know, or as far as-- I was in-- I was in my office. And I spend my time there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in-- I had my choice, as an officer, to either stay in the camp, in the Ludovica Academy, which was like a West Point, or I could go out and sleep outside. I always told them I have a girlfriend. So I got to go to my girlfriend at night. And they were-- they liked that very much, the idea, you know. And they always told me there, you should have some girlfriend for us, too. And my steady answer was always this-- if you're man enough to want one, you should be man enough to find one. [LAUGHS] And I used to tell them  this. And-- and I was able to get away with it. And that's the only reason I could go out at night with my car and take my car with me. That's the only reason. Until one time, my car was ruined, my Mercedes. And that's when I changed to a DKW, which was a Audi, because my car was ruined, you know. Granite ruined the car, which came in because the Russian shelled the city, you know. But otherwise, I had never had to do anything, anything what they call physical, except what I chose to do, you know, on my own for the underground. But anything physical-- nothing. I was issued my gun, you know. And I was issued a rifle, which I absolutely-- I mean, I was-- never even-- I was in a office. I never even-- no, I'm wrong. Afterwards, I took it down to the hiding place, put him under the work bench, just in case if my pistol misfire, I have another one, you know, in case if I have the people, you know, if anybody would come in and try to, you know, nosey.

speaker: interviewee
text: One time, a person came in. I wasn't there. My cousin Susie would tell you, if you ever have the chance to talk to her. And they were able to talk [INAUDIBLE]. They said, how come you're in  not in your home? How come you're sitting here in the basement of your factory? Said, oh, because in case of air raid, this feels safer in a-- in the basement than being home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they were able to get away. And the guys did not look around, because the hole, which was about that big, about four bricks, they were covered with all kind of a junk and everything. So people didn't know it. And when somebody came in, we always-- they always knocked on the wall. No words. Someone is here, a stranger, even if their friends came down. They-- because a lot of time, Susie had friends, and my aunt has friends. They won't even trust their friends. When they came in there, they wouldn't do-- and one day, they locked inside everything, because they were cooking, and don't want to see how many people they're cooking for, you know. So finally, somebody knocked so much, you know. And they are-- they-- finally, they had to let them in. They don't want them to be suspicious. They said, oh my god, look at all this food. It'll last for you for the month. Said, that's why we're cooking, because we just-- it's cold here. Everything will keep better. This way we have it, because you know, you can't get nothing. So this was one time when they almost gave away. And they were their friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me how you felt and what Liberation Day was like for-- for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very disappointed. I mean, we're talking about when the Russians came in? Or when the American Liberation Day?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm talking about liberation itself, when the-- when the war ended.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my god, you know. I don't think so you could ever find a happier man in the  world. I was-- I was happy-- [COUGHS] excuse me-- to be alive. Because I never expected to live that long. I never expected, to be very honest with you, to survive all of this. I-- any day when I left, I expected to be killed or if somebody will recognize me. I never expected to come back alive daily.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the liberation came, when the war was over in Europe, I mean, I could not express you the feeling how I felt. And I was just hoping, still, you know, that they lied about my father and my brother. Then they be back. And for two-- for the whole two years, I was hoping. I was hoping that somebody made a mistake. Maybe just was deported, maybe were not killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it-- it's very hard to explain you know, that day. Because I, who always loved, like I told you this, American movies, American theater, you know, and I believed in a American system-- to me, it was like born again. And I never expected to come to the United States, to be honest with you. If the Russians wouldn't have taken over Hungary, I would have stayed in Hungary. Because I had everything there. I had no reason to come here to seek my fortune. But when I had-- when they threatened me, to arrest me, I had no other choice to leave, you know, to country. But I was glad. I was very sad when Roosevelt died because I felt like, in the Bible said, you know, like Moses, you know, who-- we always compared with  him, you know, the man who-- who led the people to the Promised Land. But at the gate, he never went-- stepped through. And that's the way I felt about Roosevelt. And the same token, I find out this Roosevelt could have done so much more to save a lot more Jews, what he didn't do. I find out this later on. I find out when he turned the ship back with Jews, you know, because he would not let them immigrate here and wouldn't let them-- give them even a safe haven here. So I find this out later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- afterwards, you know, it's kind of a tarnished of his image in my eye. But nevertheless, the victory, general, it was great for me because I hated the Nazis, what they stand for, and what they did to the people. And I don't think so-- there was ever a happier day in my life than when-- when I heard of V-Day, you know. That was one of the greatest thing at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I, in the same time, I was concerned about the day there-- Russians. I was very much concerned. Because I used to say this. The Russians made two mistakes during the war. They let the Russian soldiers see the free world and the free world see the Russian soldiers. Because they behaved like animals. They raped every woman from 6 to 60.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, and-- and at the time, you know, the Russians had a big army, which fought with the German, the Vlasov army. I don't know you ever heard about it. They were the Ukrainian Army, who was with Hitler. And Eisenhower decided to give this back to the Russians, and they were all killed, you know, which I felt no bad-- bad feeling for it, you know, because what they did, you know? But I was worried about the Russians because I seen how inhuman they were, even to each other. They shot each other for a bottle of vodka. I seen them on the street, the Russians, in-- in 1945. They killed an old man because he wouldn't give up his glove. No, I felt, my god, what kind of a liberation is this? You know? And-- and I was very sure, and in my mind and in my thoughts, that nothing like this happen under the American Army, which didn't. I'm very sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, so that's the reason I was disappointed of the Yalta agreement. Because this would-- gave us a chance, in October 15, 1944, to be occupied by the English and American forces and would have been a different story. And the deportation from all these people would not have taken place. And that's the only reason I felt this Roosevelt was too feeble-minded or too sick to stand up for his right in Yalta and divide Europe at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about you for a little bit more. When and how did you dispose of your German uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did one hour after the first Russian soldier knocked on the door-- one hour after. Believe me. Because I knew, because some of our friends came down, told, the Russians are here. The Russians are here. They are here. And they told me, said, Les, everything-- the shoes, everything which has anything to do with the Germans-- get rid of it. Because you-- anything in German, they think you are a German soldier who changed clothes. And they'll take you right away back to Siberia, to the prisoner of war camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So did-- I had some clothes there. I did everything. I broke half of the rifle, so help me God. I broke in half so I can dispose of it. I took the Luger, put them in the sewer. I didn't want anything to find on me, anything, anything which resembled anything German. Because see, I did not speak Russian at the time. And the only [INAUDIBLE] speak spoke in German or Hungarian.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they did not-- they shot first and asked questions later. And did not-- I went through enough, you know, without endangering my life. After I survived this war, which I never expected to survive, I don't wanted to be killed by the people whom I, so to speak, work with, or work for. Because I work-- we work for the Allied Intelligence. We didn't for just the American or the English or France or Russian. We worked for what they called the Allied Intelligence. And-- and I just did not wanted to be killed by one of my liberators. So that's the reason I got rid of it and within an hour. If somebody would come here, he couldn't have find nothing, absolutely nothing. Because I just felt, you know, that it's-- it's no way I can explain anything, or talk to them, or they would understand it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because even today, when I talk to people, somebody has the question marks in their eyes. Were you really in the SS? How many people did you killed? Did you kill any Jews? I have questioned this too. I have very much questions. I have a very good friend of mine, an Italian fellow, Sam Shore. He wanted to write a book about me. He's the only one who believes me, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But a lot of people, you know-- we go-- Coach Majors, you know. He is my head coach, football coach. He says to me, it's Les. He was Hitler's chauffeur. [LAUGHS] I mean, they make a joke out about it, you know. And-- and that bugs the hell out of me, you know. I wasn't Hitler's chauffeur, you know. And they always ask me. They said, sure, he said. He made out with Eva Braun and things like joke-- joking about me, you know. That bugs me. Because they don't understand. And they never will understand my situation, what I went through. You know, and I don't think so too many people will understand it, what I had to go through all these years. Because, like, I could not emphasize enough, every second I could have been killed. Every move I made I-- to save somebody else, it was my death sentence if they catch me. And there was no military tribunal. If somebody catch somebody like this, the first commanding officer shot him right there, and that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's-- you had to believe and dedicated to it. I'll tell you what I-- I actually gained by this whole thing-- one watch. Would you believe it? When I-- one time, when I went for the point when taking all the people, all the Jewish, everything was there. And my watch broke. You know, it didn't run. So I just picked up a watch-- a watch which was working, and I left my watch there. That was my profit of the Second World War, one broken watch I exchange. So there's one-- I think there are six very important elements you've left out of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife and your children. Why don't you tell us the woman you married, her name, and her--

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife-- I met my wife in Germany, when I worked for the UNESCO, you know. And-- and she was, at the time, 16 years old. Her sister invited me for Christmas to their place, and I met her then. She wanted to be a nun. She would have been better off to be a nun. [LAUGHS] And we married one month before her 17th birthday, and we came to the United States. And I have five children, whom I am very proud of all of them. You know, they are pretty good kids. And my oldest daughter came with me to Budapest and met my family there. That's he's the only one-- I mean, she's the only one, she and her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell us your wife's name and your children's names.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Georgine Bohrer, B-O-H-R-E-R. Her father was a professional German soldier of they-- what they call on the [? 100,000-man ?] army, back after the First World War, in [INAUDIBLE]. And in 1931, he left the army, and he became the fire chief of the city  of Dortmund. He was a fireman. And they were bombed out from there and went-- lived in a small town in the Bavarian Alps. That's where I met her. And my son was born-- my first son was born in Germany. He was three months old when we came here. And my wife was, at the time, 18 and a half years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your oldest son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Robert Paul.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then why  don't you just tell me the rest of your children's names.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then Michael John, then Karen Mary, and Edward David, and Christina Anne. That's my children. And they're all doing pretty good, you know, really. My oldest son is an attorney. And he's a judge in a Juvenile Court of Pittsburgh. He's a kind of an appointed judge, you know, as juvenile courts are. My oldest son has a motion picture lab and a camera store. My daughter is a violinist. He was with-- a violinist with the Delaware Symphony. She was a concert [INAUDIBLE] of a symphony. Now she's with the Pennsylvania Ballet, and she works on a Broadway shows, with-- in the orchestra, with the Broadway shows. That's why I got my free tickets. [LAUGHS] Then my other son, Edward David, he is a director of out-patient services of a rehab hospital. And my youngest daughter is in Daytona Beach College. She is a counselor in a Daytona Beach College. So they're all doing better than the father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- [LAUGHS] and what about grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have two-- four-- now, wait a minute. One, two, three, four. It's correct now. Let me correct it. I got two grandchildren-- I mean, two boys, I beg your pardon-- two boys, Ian Leslie McClelland and Charles Edward McClelland. And I had two little girls. One is Kira Elizabeth Banos. That's from my oldest son-- no, from my second son. And I have Laura Banos from my oldest son. I have four grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on that note, which is a happy one, I want to thank you very, very much for taking all of this time and for coming all the way from Pittsburgh to share so much of your life with us. And we're going to photograph-- we're going to film your photographs, and then we'll be finished. Thank you very much.
